danger_signals.visual_form: Distinct creature sprites differentiated from human soldiers
danger_signals.meaning: Supernatural threat; creatures under control of Duma Faithful cult or dark forces

danger_signals.visual_form: Named unique enemy unit on map (e.g., Desaix, Grieth, Dolth)
danger_signals.meaning: Significant military/political threat; defeating them advances story and removes faction leadership

danger_signals.visual_form: Grouped human soldier sprites on grid-based battlefield
danger_signals.meaning: Organized military opposition from specific faction

danger_signals.visual_form: Specialized enemy class appearing in Chapter 2 seaborne areas
danger_signals.meaning: Lawless maritime threat; different from organized military enemies

danger_signals.visual_form: Generic enemy class in opening chapter locations
danger_signals.meaning: Lowest-tier threat; civilian-level danger from disorganized forces

safety_signals.visual_form: Designated settlement areas accessible during exploration
safety_signals.meaning: Zones where player can recruit allies, trade items, obtain healing/supplies; no combat

safety_signals.visual_form: NPCs present during battles but not attacking player party
safety_signals.meaning: Neutral or temporary ally forces; provide battlefield advantage without being player-controlled

safety_signals.visual_form: Class advancement available when unit reaches level threshold
safety_signals.meaning: System of growth and increasing capability; reinforces safety through character progression

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Associated with Earth Mother Mila deity worship
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Desaix's coup forces (distinct military faction within Zofia)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Zofia Castle as central location; western territory of Valentia

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Associated with War Father Duma deity worship
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Generic 'Rigel Army' soldiers; Emperor Rudolf as supreme leader
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Eastern territory of Valentia; Rigel Plains as distinct region; Duma Temple as spiritual center

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinct generic unit type labeled 'Desaix's Army'
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Human soldiers loyal to coup organizer rather than kingdom authority
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Present during Chapter 1 as primary threat; represent internal Zofian conflict

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinct generic unit type labeled 'Grieth's Army'
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Forces of warlord Grieth in eastern deserts
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Eastern desert region; fought during Celica's Chapter 3

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Associated with 'Netherworld Army' or 'madō' (sorcery/heresy path) forces
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Often control monster units; cult of dark magic practitioners
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Scattered throughout later maps; culminate at Duma Temple

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Zofia in internal conflict (Desaix coup d'état)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Zofia Castle surrounded by enemy forces; Desaix-branded soldiers on maps
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player controls Alm fighting against both coup forces and external Rigel invasion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rigel Army generic units appearing across maps; Emperor Rudolf as final boss
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progression through Rigel Plains and conquest of Rigel territory as story advances

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environmental consequences visible through story progression; Celica's pilgrimage motivation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Celica's narrative branch involves searching for Mila; player controls separate army

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Duma and Mila fallen into insanity
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Monster enemies increasingly prevalent; Duma Temple as corrupted spiritual center
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Late-game maps feature supernatural creature types; culminates in Duma Temple final battle

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Multiple parallel power structures (two protagonists, two armies)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Alm and Celica each lead distinct armies; separate overworld navigation for each
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turn-based gameplay where player alternates control; armies follow parallel story arcs until union at Zofia Castle

environmental_storytelling.technique: Overworld accessibility gating by story progression
environmental_storytelling.example: Additional areas of continent open as story progresses; constrains player exploration to narrative-appropriate regions

environmental_storytelling.technique: Town locations as safe harbor and exposition hubs
environmental_storytelling.example: Player can recruit allies and trade items in towns; NPCs provide dialogue revealing faction politics and character motivations

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss character placement signals political hierarchy
environmental_storytelling.example: Named unique enemies (Desaix as coup leader, Rudolf as emperor, Grieth as warlord) represent faction leadership; defeating them removes political obstacles
Zofia Kingdom worships Mila (Earth Mother); Rigel Empire worships Duma (War Father); temples and religious sites reflect faction identity

environmental_storytelling.technique: Class-based visual distinction of military organization
environmental_storytelling.example: Player units have assigned classes (fighters, priests, cavaliers, etc.) creating visual hierarchy; matches character function to visual presentation

environmental_storytelling.technique: Monster prevalence signals corruption or supernatural influence
environmental_storytelling.example: Early chapters feature mostly human enemies (bandits, soldiers); later chapters introduce monsters under Duma Faithful control

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Leads army against Desaix coup and Rigel invasion; fights to protect Zofia; positioned as idealistic warrior
Discovered to be Rudolf's son; motivation shifts to understanding father's true intentions; invades Rigel to confront emperor
Embarks on pilgrimage to find Mila; separate from Alm's conflict; leads own army; driven by religious/spiritual duty
Reunites with Alm at Zofia Castle but parts ways over disagreement about peaceful resolution with Rudolf
Leads military conquest across Valentia; positioned as antagonist; reveals on deathbed that invasion aimed to create military champion against insane deities
Military leader organizing internal coup against Zofia; primary threat in Chapter 1; represents faction instability
Independent warlord controlling eastern desert region; boss fought during Celica's Chapter 3; represents fragmented power structure outside main factions
War Father corrupted by extreme power; controls monsters and Duma Faithful; final supernatural antagonist defeated at Duma Temple
Organized formation movement; follow emperor's orders; positioned as legitimate military threat distinct from bandits or monsters
Disorganized threat; lower tactical capability than organized military; represent lowest-tier danger in hierarchy
Controlled by Duma Faithful; increasingly prevalent; represent supernatural corruption; higher threat than human soldiers

notes: Fire Emblem Gaiden communicates world-state primarily through faction-affiliated enemy types, story-driven narrative reveals, and overworld accessibility gating. The dual-protagonist structure (Alm and Celica leading separate armies) visualizes political division; their eventual union represents continental unification. Deity symbolism (Mila=Zofia, Duma=Rigel) embeds theological-political conflict into faction identity. Monster presence signals supernatural corruption and Duma Faithful influence, increasing in later chapters. Boss character placement marks faction leadership visually. The game lacks explicit color-coding or heraldic symbols documented in sources; faction identity relies on named generic unit types (Rigel Army, Desaix's Army, Grieth's Army) and story context rather than visual heraldry.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Fire Emblem Gaiden article)
